content.text: is a technique that stores LLM responses and retrieves them when a new query is

to a cached one — even if the exact wording differs. Instead of comparing raw strings, it converts queries into dense vector embeddings and uses cosine similarity to decide whether two questions ask the same thing.

The key insight is that "What is the best Python ORM?" and "Which Python ORM should I choose?" carry the same intent. An exact-match cache misses the second query entirely. A semantic cache returns the same high-quality answer for both.

LLM inference is expensive and slow. In production systems where many users ask overlapping questions — customer support bots, coding assistants, FAQ widgets, document Q&A — the same semantic intent recurs constantly in different surface forms. Paying full token cost and waiting 2–30 seconds for each of those requests is wasteful when a cached answer already exists.

Semantic caching sits between the user and the LLM and intercepts semantically redundant calls before they reach the model. At scale, this directly determines whether an AI product's unit economics are viable.
cache hits generate zero tokens. A 70–80% hit rate on a customer-support workload can cut monthly LLM spend by 50–70%.
a cache hit returns in 5–20 ms versus 1–30 seconds for a real LLM call. Users perceive this as instant.
serving cache hits requires no GPU time. You can handle traffic spikes without provisioning more LLM capacity.
semantically equivalent questions always receive the same vetted answer, eliminating the token-sampling variation that causes LLMs to produce slightly different outputs for identical prompts.
fewer real API calls mean less exposure to provider rate limits and throttling errors.
cached answers do not update when the underlying data or model changes. A TTL policy is mandatory; without it, the cache serves outdated information indefinitely.

the similarity cutoff (e.g. 0.92) is a tuneable knob with real tradeoffs. Too high: almost no hits. Too low: semantically distinct questions share the wrong answer. The right value is workload-specific and must be measured on real queries.
every query must be embedded before the similarity check. This is cheap (OpenAI
costs $0.02 per 1M tokens) but adds 20–80 ms latency even on cache misses.

two questions can be textually similar but factually different in context. "What is the capital of Georgia (the US state)?" and "What is the capital of Georgia (the country)?" have similar embeddings but different correct answers.
cached responses from one user must never be surfaced to another user if the answer is user-specific or contains private data. Cache namespacing by user or tenant is required.
a fresh cache has zero hit rate. The cache must warm up — either from historical queries or by pre-populating with FAQ pairs — before delivering significant savings.
The core pipeline for every incoming query:
convert the incoming text to a dense vector (e.g. 1536 dimensions for OpenAI embeddings).
query the vector store for the most similar cached embedding using cosine similarity.
if the top result's similarity score >= threshold (e.g. 0.92), it is a
if no result clears the threshold, forward the query to the LLM. Wait for the response.
store the (embedding, response) pair in the vector store with a TTL. Return the response to the user.
The embedding model used at query time must be the
used when storing responses, otherwise vector distances are meaningless. Never mix embedding models across a cache.

attrs.alt: Semantic caching architecture: query is embedded, checked against vector cache with cosine similarity, returns cached response on hit or calls LLM on miss then stores result

content.text: Semantic caching provides the most value when queries exhibit
and responses have a reasonable shelf life. Ideal scenarios:
— users ask the same product questions in different words daily.
— a finite knowledge base with well-bounded answer space.
— "How do I open a file in Python?" recurs across millions of sessions.
— queries about category or feature comparisons cluster tightly.
— global cache (for public knowledge) combined with per-tenant cache (for private data).
when queries are highly personalized ("What should I eat tonight given my health history?"), time-sensitive ("What is the current price of AAPL?"), or require fresh retrieval from live data sources.
Choose an embedding model and fix it.
(1536 dims) is a solid default. Record which model version you use — a version change invalidates the entire cache.
works for simple use cases. Qdrant or Pinecone scale better for millions of cached entries with ANN indexing.
Implement the cache layer as middleware.
Wrap your LLM call: embed the query, search the store, return if above threshold, else call the LLM, then write back.
Start with 24–72 hours for general knowledge. Shorten for time-sensitive topics (news, prices). Use per-entry TTL if different query types age at different rates.
Include the system prompt hash or tenant ID in the cache key space. Responses authored for a technical-support system prompt must not be served when the active system prompt is a children's tutor.

Run 200–500 representative query pairs through the embedding model, label whether they deserve the same answer, and plot the similarity score distribution. Find the threshold that yields an acceptable false-positive rate (< 1–2%) before going to production.
Log cache hit rate, similarity scores, and (if feasible) user satisfaction signals for cache-hit responses vs. live responses. Alert if quality diverges.
Minimal custom semantic cache with OpenAI

import json
import hashlib
from openai import OpenAI
from typing import Optional

client = OpenAI()

# --- In-memory cache for illustration; replace with Redis/Qdrant in production ---
_cache: list[dict] = []  # [{"embedding": [...], "response": "...", "query": "..."}]


def _embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model="text-embedding-3-small",
        input=text,
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding


def _cosine_similarity(a: list[float], b: list[float]) -> float:
    dot = sum(x * y for x, y in zip(a, b))
    mag_a = sum(x**2 for x in a) ** 0.5
    mag_b = sum(x**2 for x in b) ** 0.5
    return dot / (mag_a * mag_b) if mag_a and mag_b else 0.0


def semantic_cache_lookup(query: str, threshold: float = 0.92) -> Optional[str]:
    """Return a cached response if a semantically similar query was answered before."""
    query_emb = _embed(query)
    best_score = 0.0
    best_response = None
    for entry in _cache:
        score = _cosine_similarity(query_emb, entry["embedding"])
        if score > best_score:
            best_score = score
            best_response = entry["response"]
    if best_score >= threshold:
        print(f"Cache HIT (score={best_score:.3f})")
        return best_response
    print(f"Cache MISS (best score={best_score:.3f})")
    return None


def ask(query: str, threshold: float = 0.92) -> str:
    cached = semantic_cache_lookup(query, threshold)
    if cached:
        return cached

    # Real LLM call on cache miss
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )
    answer = response.choices[0].message.content

    # Store for future semantic matches
    _cache.append({"embedding": _embed(query), "response": answer, "query": query})
    return answer


# Demo
print(ask("What is the best Python ORM?"))
print(ask("Which Python ORM should I choose?"))   # should hit the cache
print(ask("Recommend a Python database library"))  # may or may not hit depending on threshold
Production-grade semantic cache with GPTCache and Qdrant

# pip install gptcache qdrant-client openai

from gptcache import cache
from gptcache.adapter import openai as cached_openai
from gptcache.embedding import OpenAI as OpenAIEmbedding
from gptcache.manager import CacheBase, VectorBase, get_data_manager
from gptcache.similarity_evaluation.distance import SearchDistanceEvaluation

# 1. Configure the embedding model
embedding_model = OpenAIEmbedding()  # uses text-embedding-ada-002 by default

# 2. Configure storage: SQLite for response text, Qdrant for vectors
data_manager = get_data_manager(
    CacheBase("sqlite"),          # stores (query, response) pairs
    VectorBase(                    # stores embeddings for ANN search
        "qdrant",
        dimension=embedding_model.dimension,
        url="http://localhost:6333",
        collection_name="llm_cache",
    ),
)

# 3. Initialise the cache
cache.init(
    embedding_func=embedding_model.to_embeddings,
    data_manager=data_manager,
    similarity_evaluation=SearchDistanceEvaluation(),  # cosine distance
)
cache.set_openai_key()

# 4. Use the cache-aware OpenAI client — same API, caching is transparent
response = cached_openai.ChatCompletion.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is semantic caching?"}],
    temperature=0,  # deterministic output improves cache consistency
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Second call with a semantically similar query hits the cache
response2 = cached_openai.ChatCompletion.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain what semantic caching means"}],
    temperature=0,
)
print(response2.choices[0].message.content)  # returned from cache in ~10 ms
Async semantic cache layer with Redis Vector Search

# pip install redisvl openai

import asyncio
import json
import numpy as np
from redisvl.index import AsyncSearchIndex
from redisvl.query import VectorQuery
from redisvl.schema import IndexSchema
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

client = AsyncOpenAI()

SCHEMA = IndexSchema.from_dict({
    "index": {"name": "sem_cache", "prefix": "cache"},
    "fields": [
        {"name": "query", "type": "text"},
        {"name": "response", "type": "text"},
        {
            "name": "embedding",
            "type": "vector",
            "attrs": {
                "dims": 1536,
                "distance_metric": "cosine",
                "algorithm": "hnsw",   # Hierarchical Navigable Small World — fast ANN
            },
        },
    ],
})


async def get_embedding(text: str) -> list[float]:
    r = await client.embeddings.create(model="text-embedding-3-small", input=text)
    return r.data[0].embedding


async def cached_llm_call(
    index: AsyncSearchIndex,
    query: str,
    threshold: float = 0.92,
) -> str:
    emb = await get_embedding(query)
    emb_bytes = np.array(emb, dtype=np.float32).tobytes()

    # Vector similarity search
    q = VectorQuery(
        vector=emb_bytes,
        vector_field_name="embedding",
        return_fields=["query", "response", "vector_distance"],
        num_results=1,
    )
    results = await index.query(q)

    if results and (1 - float(results[0]["vector_distance"])) >= threshold:
        print(f"Cache HIT: {results[0]['query']!r}")
        return results[0]["response"]

    # Cache miss: call the LLM
    resp = await client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": query}],
    )
    answer = resp.choices[0].message.content

    # Write to cache
    await index.load([{
        "query": query,
        "response": answer,
        "embedding": emb_bytes,
    }])
    return answer

A B2B SaaS company runs a customer support chatbot backed by Claude. Their product has ~200 distinct features, and users ask roughly 1,500 distinct questions about them — but the company receives 80,000 support queries per month. Without a cache, every query hits the LLM API at an average cost of $0.012 per conversation (input + output tokens).

After deploying semantic caching with a 0.91 similarity threshold and a 48-hour TTL, their cache warm-up period (first 48 hours) ends with ~1,500 canonical questions stored. Thereafter, roughly 73% of incoming queries hit the cache. Their monthly LLM cost drops from ~$960 to ~$260 — a 73% reduction — and median response latency falls from 4.2 s to 0.3 s.

# Simplified production setup used by the B2B company above
# Namespace by tenant so TechCorp's cache doesn't bleed into OtherCorp's

import hashlib

class TenantAwareSemanticCache:
    def __init__(self, vector_store, embedding_fn, threshold: float = 0.91, ttl_seconds: int = 172800):
        self.store = vector_store
        self.embed = embedding_fn
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.ttl = ttl_seconds

    def _namespace(self, tenant_id: str, system_prompt: str) -> str:
        """Combine tenant ID and system prompt hash to isolate cache partitions."""
        prompt_hash = hashlib.sha256(system_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()[:8]
        return f"{tenant_id}:{prompt_hash}"

    def lookup(self, tenant_id: str, system_prompt: str, user_query: str):
        ns = self._namespace(tenant_id, system_prompt)
        query_emb = self.embed(user_query)
        result = self.store.search(query_emb, namespace=ns, top_k=1)
        if result and result[0].score >= self.threshold:
            return result[0].payload["response"]
        return None

    def store_response(self, tenant_id: str, system_prompt: str, user_query: str, response: str):
        ns = self._namespace(tenant_id, system_prompt)
        query_emb = self.embed(user_query)
        self.store.upsert(query_emb, payload={"response": response}, namespace=ns, ttl=self.ttl)
a threshold of 0.70 returns semantically adjacent but factually distinct answers. The classic failure is returning the answer about state Georgia when the user asked about country Georgia.
cached answers about an API's behavior or a product's pricing become dangerously stale. Every cache entry must have a TTL matched to how often that class of information changes.
, stored embeddings from the old model are in a different vector space. You must flush the entire cache and re-warm from scratch.
Ignoring the system prompt in cache keys:

the same user question receives a different answer depending on the active system prompt (formal vs. casual tone, English vs. Spanish, admin role vs. guest role). Omitting the system prompt from the cache namespace causes cross-contamination.
questions about current time, live prices, or the user's own account state cannot be cached. Add a pre-filter that routes obviously time-sensitive or personalized queries directly to the LLM.
Not measuring quality of cache hits:
a high cache hit rate is only valuable if hit quality is good. Log thumbs-up/thumbs-down feedback separately for cached vs. live responses and track divergence.
Run threshold calibration on real traffic
before launch: sample 500 query pairs, manually label same/different intent, and plot F1 score vs. threshold to find the operating point.
(or the closest deterministic setting) when storing responses. Caching a stochastic response and serving it verbatim for thousands of queries amplifies any defects in that single sample.
— do not block the response to the user while writing to the vector store. Use a background task or message queue for the cache-write step.
— static reference content ("What does API term X mean?") can be cached for weeks; dynamic operational content ("How do I check my current usage?") should be cached for hours at most.
— seed the cache from historical support tickets or documentation before going live to avoid the cold-start period.
in your vector store (supported by Qdrant, Redis Stack, Weaviate). It provides sub-millisecond ANN search at millions of entries without full-scan overhead.
— when product documentation changes, provide an admin endpoint or script that deletes cache entries by tag or prefix, rather than waiting for TTL expiration.
The dominant latency on cache hits is the embedding call (~20–80 ms for a single short query via the OpenAI API). Reducing this is the main performance lever:
or FastEmbed) on-CPU takes 2–8 ms and costs nothing per query. Accuracy is slightly lower than OpenAI's models but often sufficient for support workloads.
if building a pre-populating pipeline (e.g. embedding all historical support tickets), submit queries in batches of 100–2048 to the embedding API rather than one at a time.
at fewer than 50,000 cached entries, exact search (brute-force cosine over all vectors) is fast enough. Above 100,000 entries, switch to an HNSW or IVF index to keep search under 5 ms.
Qdrant and Redis can be deployed as distributed clusters. The semantic cache layer is stateless — multiple application instances share one vector store with no coordination overhead.
a 1536-dimension float32 embedding occupies 6 KB. One million cached entries require ~6 GB RAM for embeddings alone plus storage for response text. Size your vector store accordingly.
Drop-in wrapper for OpenAI SDK; pluggable backends
Excellent for teams already on Redis; HNSW supported
Payload filtering, named collections, Rust core for speed
Serverless pricing; metadata filtering; no ops overhead
Built-in semantic cache module; GraphQL query API
ONNX-based; 2–8 ms per query; no API cost
Broad model selection; MTEB leaderboard guidance
Wraps LLM calls with a configurable cache backend

to prompt prefix caching. Use the provider's prompt prefix cache (which reduces input token cost for shared system prompts) at the model layer, and semantic caching (which avoids the call entirely) at the application layer.

if one user's query contains personal data that gets stored in the cache (e.g., "What is the refund status for order #ABC123?"), a semantically similar query from a different user could retrieve it. Sanitize or redact PII from queries before embedding and storing, or namespace the cache to prevent cross-user retrieval.
a malicious user could craft a query that gets cached with an injected payload, which is then served to other users. Validate and sanitize both queries and LLM responses before caching.

if an attacker can write directly to the vector store or force specific entries via a crafted sequence of queries, they can serve arbitrary responses to downstream users. Restrict write access to the cache layer to the application service only.

decide upfront whether to serve stale cache hits when the LLM API is down ("stale-on-fail" pattern) or to fail open. Serving stale responses may be acceptable for FAQ bots but not for workflows where freshness is critical.
Reliability of the cache store itself:

the vector store is now in the critical path. It must be deployed with replication and a health check. On cache-store failure, the system should gracefully degrade by routing all queries directly to the LLM rather than returning an error.
Semantic caching stores LLM responses indexed by embedding and retrieves them when cosine similarity to a new query exceeds a threshold — matching intent rather than exact wording.
A 70–80% cache hit rate is achievable on repetitive workloads (support bots, FAQ systems) and can reduce LLM API costs by 50–70% and median latency from seconds to milliseconds.

The similarity threshold is the most critical parameter: calibrate it on labeled real query pairs before launch, and monitor it continuously. Too low causes wrong-answer false positives; too high undermines the cache benefit.
Always namespace the cache by tenant ID and system prompt hash to prevent response cross-contamination between users, roles, and product contexts.
TTLs are mandatory: every cache entry must expire. Match TTL length to how frequently that class of information changes in the real world.
Never switch embedding models without flushing the entire cache: embeddings from different models are incomparable.
Self-hosted embedding models (FastEmbed, sentence-transformers) eliminate per-embedding API cost and reduce the cache hit latency to 2–8 ms on CPU.
Semantic caching is complementary to, not a replacement for, prompt prefix caching (which reduces input token cost at the model level) and KV cache (which is transparent and provider-managed).
